I tend to think that the `shlex.split()` approach is appropriate for `UJSON_BUILD_DC_LIBS`, since `LDFLAGS`-style environment variables are usually like `-L/usr/lib/foo -lbar -lbat`. There’s some risk of surprising magic by doing this, but it pretty closely resembles the behavior of traditional build systems that end up doing something like `${CC} ${CFLAGS} ${LDFLAGS} …` with unquoted shell interpolation.

```python
shlex.split(environ.get("UJSON_BUILD_DC_LIBS", ""))
```

Meanwhile, the proposed `UJSON_BUILD_DC_INCLUDES` is a list-of-paths like `PATH` rather than a set of `CFLAGS`-style compiler flags, so splitting on `os.pathsep`, i.e. `:`, does seem reasonable there.

```python
environ.get("UJSON_BUILD_DC_INCLUDES", "./deps/double-conversion/double-conversion").split(pathsep)
```

UTF-8 string literals in C++ can be created by appending a u8 to a normal string literal. So for example en empty UTF-8 string can be created as follows
```C++
std::string word = u8"";
```
A non-UTF-8 empty string can be created as follows
```C++
std::string word = "";
```
which is what is done above.

The question is what happens when we append UTF-8 strings to a non-UTF-8 string
```C++
word.append(item.character);
```
as we are currently doing in this code.

This code (and the corresponding serialization code) is also subtle.

`deserialize_property`, when called on the `UNKNOWN` sentinel, produces None. If value is `None`, that implies that it was unknown to the engine. By not adding it to the output dictionary, when we resolve all of the outstanding futures on a resource when `RegisterResource` returns, we'll report that an output is known if its value is not None. So, from this direction, everything works fine - user code never observes UNKNOWN because applies on unknown outputs don't run during previews.

From the serialization direction, all resources initialize their output properties to the value `None`, so if they are not provided they are not known to the SDK when calling `RegisterResource` and it does not make sense to include them in the `RegisterResource` RPC call.

Although `BatchCommand.from_tracked_request` has a couple defensive pathways to return `None`, I don't think they are actually possible:

* The first condition is that all spans in `complete_spans` have a non-`None` `end_time`, but this is always the case given that spans go through `stop()` before being added to `complete_spans`
* The second condition is that the request has a non-`None` `end_time`, but this is checked and set just a couple lines above in `TrackedRequest.finish()`

Also this is the only call site, and there are many other fields within tracked request/span that if not correct would lead to a bad message being sent to the core agent.

Therefore I've removed the defensive pathways.

So all of the `modin.numpy` methods are simply wrappers that internally call `modin.numpy.array.<method>`. E.g.:
```python
def add(...):
x1 = try_convert_from_interoperable_type(x1)
if isinstance(x1, array):
x1.add ...
```
So we need to make sure that the first operand is converted from a modin pandas object to a modin numpy object so that it can call the appropriate method, but inside the `modin.numpy.array.add` method, the first line attempts to convert the other operand to an array.

Take `__add__` as an example. On [this](https://github.com/RehanSD/modin/blob/a94b84cd6ebc63a0d951476ee1fd34629ae7c6de/modin/numpy/arr.py#L868) line, we call `_binary_op` - `_binary_op`, makes sure that the second operand is of type array, and errors if it isn't on these lines: https://github.com/RehanSD/modin/blob/a94b84cd6ebc63a0d951476ee1fd34629ae7c6de/modin/numpy/arr.py#L634-L638 - so we don't need to convert the second operand at the numpy layer.

Maybe one can create a package with grammar library with lots of different grammar files. Then you can add that package to your own module where you also have a somewhat complex grammar split over multiple files. (Or a plugin system). If you try to use relative imports, we have to prevent the grammars from one package to find the grammars in the other package.

Under the old file-based system, this wasn't a problem since we always specified an absolute file path that clearly defines which grammar file we are talking about.

But now we can't easily do that. If we just use `full_path` there might be ambiguity of both packages use the `grammars` folder. If we try to use an absolute path, this will break when we are inside of a zip file. So we somehow have to encode both the package and the path inside of the package, unless we don't want to support those usecases. And I don't see any real harm done by this.

Yeah, I suspect if we're not going to dirty them, we shouldn't allow deps; I'm imagining something like (I'm aware we don't support non-cacheable `@rule`s _now_, but we should make the framework force us to think about it if we add them):
```python
@rule(cacheable=False)
def foo():
  executable = yield Get[Snapshot](some_path_globs)
  do_sideeffecting_thing_with(executable)
```

where the side-effecting rule has a dep, someone edits the snapshotted file, we re-snapshot because that was invalidated, but we don't re-run the rule because it's side-effecting. Erroring out (ideally on the first `get` from the side-effecting rule, rather than on invalidation), rather than silently not running the rule, feels safer/less surprising.

In `resources_from_ray_options`, `CPU` will never be `None`. The `CPU` key will be removed if the input is `None`.

https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/e402fc0eaa07d3dd396730661bb799e5aab9a03a/python/ray/_private/utils.py#L307-L349

Also Ray does not set `num_cpus=None` automatically if it is not specified by users, after recent updates.

This design was to differentiate "default CPU value" and "CPU value not set". Not differentiating "default value" and "value not set" is a very bad idea, because 1. you cannot catch the bug that you forget to set a default value 2. it confuses the dependent code who want to change the options, because it does not know whether the value was set initially. For example: https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/24131/files

> I don't think just mentioning execution plan will mean performance or value added automatically -- it's just a standard thing across frameworks.

I was juxtaposing talking about a query/execution planner in high-level docs vs API-level docs, I know that just mentioning a planner won't automatically convey what I mentioned. But the query/execution planner is often discussed in landing-page/concepts/user guide docs as (1) a fundamental concept that can help the user to understand how their query is executed, and (2) a way to talk about their performance optimizations, among other reasons.

>Even SQL would have a statement like EXPLAIN PLAN as part of the language for users.

That's an API for explaining the execution plan, which would obviously deal with the execution plan. But other APIs that don't need to reference the execution plan, shouldn't for the sake of not needlessly confusing the user, right? I don't think I'm saying anything too controversial here.

>The rest are just general principles of API design -- those are separate topics from whether lineage/execution plan is leaky.

I think that each are relevant to this general API discussion, but we agree that including the lineage concept is not leaky, so we don't have to go into that.

>That's why there is "as/to" in API name, so that it can apply to eager datasets as well.

So I think where we're seeing this differently is that I don't consider lineage-based serialization to be converting the dataset to a lazy dataset. The dataset's execution mode will be maintained upon deserialization, e.g. if it's an eager dataset before lineage-based serialization, it will be eager after deserialization, so it's not really converting to or casting as a lazy dataset. I consider lineage as distinctly separate from execution mode.

I considered that option, but I came to the conclusion that it is not possible unless we modify the `otTcpEndpoint` structure. 

To understand why, consider the case where the circular buffer installs its own `otTcpForwardProgressCallback`. When that happens, the callback is passed a pointer to the `otTcpEndpoint`. There's no way for the body of the callback to convert that into a pointer to the corresponding `otTcpCircularSendBuffer` structure. This could be fixed by adding an extra pointer in `otTcpEndpoint`, but that is a more complex change.

I'm still happy to add a new element to `otTcpEndpoint` to support the paradigm you mentioned if you feel it's better. But I preferred the current approach---have the user call a function at the top of the callback---as I think it is simpler.

`&input` here points to an array in `info.inputs_`, which is `std::vector<NDArray> inputs_`. Ie. it contains proper NDArrays and not only pointers to NDArrays that could become invalid.
We control the lifetime of the NDArrays in `std::vector<NDArray> inputs_`. They are copies, created based on the ndarray handle passed when invoking an operator and created in in `DCInfo::Create`. The copies are destroyed here in `DCInfo::Compute` after computation finished.

Note that the copies share the same underlying storage chunk. The storage chunk is deallocated once the frontend doesn't hold any handle to an NDArray owning that chunk anymore and once all copies located in DCInfo.inputs_ are destroyed.

The combination of the project layout and the kinds of tests we run is rather unique (2 top-level packages, in-tree tests and flat-layout, tests using pip-run), which require a bespoke path configuration...

For most of packages using src-layout, the following configuration is enough:
```ini
[paths]
source =
	src/
	*/site-packages/
```
The equivalent for flat-layout is:
```ini
[paths]
source =
	../  # seems to be resolved against the `.coveragerc` file path, not the current folder
	*/site-packages/
```

... but `coverage` does not allow specifying wildcards as the last segment in `[paths]`, therefore we need an entry for each `setuptools` and `pkg_resources`.
`*/pip-install-*` paths seem to be created by `pip-run` and `*/tmp*` paths are created when installing into directories created with the `tempfile` module.

I think there is an expectation we've created for nimi-python users that you can use a context manager, and it would be bad not to support it in nitclk.

```
with nitclk.initiate(session_reference_list)
   # do stuff
# more code after aborting
```

The difference is that we'd need a context manager that on `__exit__` iterates over the `nitclk.SessionReference` objects and calls `abort()` on them. Which means `nitclk.SessionReference` objects need to internally hold on to `nifoo.Session`. But with duck-typing it should all "just work" I believe.

* either this is a logic run specifically on RHEL 6, and for the same reasons you explained, dedicated tests should be added
* either this is a global logic, testable from another OS, but this is not that great because we are not testing the real case, and the increase of regressions as side-effects is increased for other systems.

You are saying that RHEL 6, SL 6 and OL 6 are a very small portion of our users. I think the support we propose here must be in balance with it. That said, without explicit dedicated tests, we should choose the most pratical solutions for these users. That's correct, but that's not the usual way of this pattern. Should look more like this:
```python
request_id = unset = _Unset()
....
if request_id is not unset:
   ....
```
you could even not use a class at all
```python
request_id = unset = object()
....
if request_id is not unset:
   ....
```
The official pattern is to create a unique pointer in memory, and because it's a pointer you can use the "is" keyword to compare it's the same object in memory. That's one of the rare scenario when you can check using "is", since you could check that the instance in memory is the same, instead of checking "isinstance" (which is way more expensive than testing a simple pointer comparison).

Second, it was not directly clear to me, that this is not a `bytes` instance, but rather a `hexbytes.HexBytes` instance, that is returned from web3.

Where the difference is in hex encoding:

```python
from hexbytes import HexBytes

b = b'6\x03\xe4Ne[lth\xd5\xd2#\xddw\xfd\x15\xa9i=\xd57\x8b\xf0Mf\xfe\xc1X\xf1\x7f\xb3\xeb'
hb = HexBytes(b)

print(hb.hex())
print(b.hex())
```

The test covers all of the potential race scenarios that I uncovered. The main race is exposed when the logger is invoked at a rate sufficiently fast enough (which is not all that fast in actuality). In that scenario, the buffer would be drained while it was being written to by other threads. The other race is that one we were discussing previously during shutdown. The test covers the former case by sending a high volume of logs (10k) as quickly as possible, stressing the access synchronization to the buffer, and covers the latter case by shutting down the logger at the end and then subsequently verifying that all expected data was emitted by the logger.

> Issue 1: If we just pass these to __init__() then calling from_dict() could raise either a ValueError or a TypeError, which means that we're potentially going to have to catch more types of errors in the utils methods, which means there's less precision and a greater chance we accidentally mask a programming error.

Hmm, so it's about working around the fact that python treats a missing constructor argument as a TypeError (paired with the desire to have utils suppress errors on init). I hadn't thought about this line.

> Issue 2: As a programmer manually instantiating this, you may want/need more flexibility, but there is higher trust here that you "know what you are doing" and it's not really the constructor's job to prevent you from instantiating at all (e.g., you might continue to modify these fields after instantiation and make it valid). If you are calling from_dict the assumption is that you're parsing something you've received from another service and trying to see if it's a proper session error, and in this case we act as strictly as possible.

I'm less sympathetic to this line of thinking.
Reducing the verification done in construction reduces the guarantee of anyone interacting with the object.

If we want to allow users to build up an object which they finalize into a materialized object (which I'm not sure if we do), we should keep verification in the constructor but expose a builder pattern to explicitly defer verificiation until a certain point, not make our constructor a faux-builder while dropping it's verification:

```python
GlobusSessionError.builder()\
   .code("MyCoolError")\
   .session_authorization_parameter(parameter)\
   .build()
```

1. The one Stephen mentioned above - multiple paths to reason about lead to orphaned code (we update one path but not the other)

It becomes useful for running tests in sample app when the user creates their own admin class, say they make `sample_x509.CaAdmin` and register it, i.e `Ca` is registered with `sample_x509.CaAdmin`.
Now, When running tests, in `tests/test_admin.py` contains line `from ..admin import CaAdmin, CertAdmin` which calls the `django_x509/admin.py` file, since it contains: `admin.site.register(Ca, CaAdmin)`; it tries to register `Ca` with `django_x509.CaAdmin`.
This raises: `django.contrib.admin.sites.AlreadyRegistered: The model Ca is already registered with 'sample_x509.CaAdmin'.`

To avoid this, I added this check, to check for `Ca` being already registered, if it is, we don't try to register again.

In all other cases, the statements are doing nothing.

> You seemed to be on board with a feature flag ealier - does that still seem reasonable?

Yes, absolutely! Pip still has the zip file magic I proposed to them behind `--use-feature=fast-deps` for similar reasons, although that's had a lot more people contribute to it since then.

> the new cache burden (which is you thankfully eliminated for --layout packed users like Pants, but still exists for folks who never use(d) that option).

Yes: for this reason alone I think it should be behind a feature flag.

> Ideally the original tack of a library that handled the zipping, possibly including parallelization in addition to the ability here of merging would take the place of all this to both eliminate the hack factor and speed things up in the cold cache case not to mention make these features available outside Pex and outside Python.

Well yes, that definitely hasn't evaporated; in fact, it's quite useful, and indeed takes advantage of rust's structured parallelism, and it's a CLI, a rust library, and a pyo3 python library: https://github.com/cosmicexplorer/medusa-zip. I also got the hang of using github actions and cibuildwheels in the meantime and it's now published to crates.io (`{,lib,py}medusa-zip`) and pypi (`medusa-zip`) upon every tagged release. I've been continuing to speak with @jonjohnsonjr regarding the similarity of this work to Chainguard's use case efficiently merging tarballs and/or containers (see chainguard-dev/apko#781) and considering whether we can share any code. `libarchive` exists, but its goal is compatibility with the widest range of formats, not performance, and it seems plausible that a general framework for efficiently merging the contents of archives might have applicability to a wide range of build tools and package managers.

I don't think that can happen unless the set is internally using some data structure that makes that replacement and the GIL doesn't ensure a consistent view of it. For sets, `|=` and `-=` operate on the same `set` object, as evidenced by the fact that we can see their results through other references:

```
>>> a = set()
>>> b = a
>>> a |= set([1, 2, 3])
>>> a
set([1, 2, 3])
>>> b
set([1, 2, 3])
>>> a -= set([2])
>>> b
set([1, 3])
>>> 
```

So there's no new `set` at the Python level.

But it's never that nice to rely on the GIL; we might want to rely on some kind of synchronized queue to pass these things around between threads, or just add some locks around the set accesses.

The reason we have `wrap_like` the way it is, is that we use it inside `__torch_function__`:

https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/9ebf10af02aeb882bd8d7782149e21b48528d562/torchvision/datapoints/_datapoint.py#L32-L39

Basically, we need a generic way for "Here is a datapoint input possibly with some metadata. Given a plain tensor, make it the same type as the datapoint and copy the metadata if available".

So the reason we have `other` in all of them is because if we need metadata and pass nothing explicitly, we need to take it from `other`. This also means that on datapoints that have no metadata, we never use `other`.

Interesting approach. I have some suggestions for making it more consistent with the other tests.

One minor issue is that `python` may not actually find the right `python` command, e.g., if the user started the test using `python3` or something like that. To handle this kind of thing in other places in the codebase, we use `sys.executable`.

Take a look at how the tests are written in e.g., https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/master/python/ray/tests/test_multi_node.py

- This uses the `call_ray_start` pytest fixture to start a Ray cluster in the background.
- Then we can connect the test as a driver to the cluster with `ray.init(redis_address=...)`
- Then we can start several drivers in the background that do some printing (both to stdout and stderr) using `run_string_as_driver` or `run_string_as_driver_nonblocking` (2 or three should be sufficient).
- We can capture all of the main test scripts stdout/stderr using `with CaptureOutputAndError` like in `test_logging_to_driver`.
- We can run some tasks from the main script that do some logging.
- We can make sure that we do see the output from the tasks that the main script submitted. And make sure that we don't see the output from the tasks that the background drivers submitted.

When we pass it to HDK,  `target_type` can be used to specify a type for the `NULL` literal. But in HDK we need all 7 fields of a literal to present for correct parsing, so this particular path probably works only because we process JSON in Calcite which can add some additional casts, etc. As a general rule:
`type`, `scale`, `precision` - define a type of a value stored in the `literal` field. Any type can be used if `literal` is NULL.
`target_type`, `type_scale`, `type_precision` - define a type of the constant expression we want to have in HDK IR

(Abbreviating as “`TLCDL`” and “`_TLCDL`” throughout.) Note that while
`TLCDL` is a type (an interface), `_TLCDL` is not a type but rather a
type constructor: that is, for any type `M`, `_TLCDL<M>` is a type that
implements `TLCDL`.  Each type `_TLCDL<M>` happens to have a member
`setSeriesMetadata` whose type depends on `M`, but since the interface
of `TLCDL` does not declare any such member, the method has no bearing
on the interface. It’s just an implementation detail, like any of the
other private methods.

This suffices because all call sites of `setSeriesMetadata` already type
the receiver as `any` (naturally, since this interface is new). I wrote
it this way to avoid the clutter of the type parameter, but if you
prefer I could try to expose its intended-public API more fully?

- `path.name` instead of `os.path.split(path)[-1]`
- `path.stem` instead of `os.path.splitext(os.path.split(path)[-1])[0]`
- `path.read_bytes()` to get the file contents in a single call without needing to use `open` as a context manager.

How about the following refactor of this logic?

```python
class CompletionProgressBarView(LoginRequiredMixin, TemplateView):
    """
    View to display the progress bar of a student in a course
    """
    def __get_user_completion(username, chapter_id, results):
        """
        Return the user completion percentage, using the completion response.

        In case the user completion cannot be returned as a result of missing user completion, we return None,
        indicating its absence.
        """
        user_completion = next(filter(results, lambda r: r.get('username') == username), None)

        # No completion returned, hence we cannot get the percentage either
        # Indicate no user completion by returning None
        if not user_completion:
            return None

        chapters = user_completion['chapter']
        chapter = next(filter(chapters, lambda c: c['block_key'].split('@')[-1] == chapter_id), None)

        completion_kind = chapter if chapter else user_completion
        return float(completion_kind['completion']['percent']) * 100

    @xframe_options_exempt
    def get(self, request, course_key, chapter_id=None):
        """
        Fetch progress and render the template.
        """
        completion_percentage = 0
        completion_resp = CompletionDetailView.as_view()(request, course_key).data

        if chapter_id is not None:
            new_req = request.GET.copy()
            new_req['requested_fields'] = "chapter"
            request.GET = new_req

        if completion_resp:
            results = completion_resp.get('results')
            user_completion_percentage = self.__get_user_completion(request.user.username, chapter_id, results)

            if user_completion_percentage:
                completion_percentage = user_completion_percentage

        template = 'chapter_completion_progress_bar.html' if chapter_id is not None else 'completion_progress_bar.html'
        return render(request, template, {
            'completion_percentage': completion_percentage,
        })
```

@jianoaix The `ray.data.read_parquet()` API is more of a distributed analog for `pyarrow.parquet.ParquetDataset`, where we expose certain features that the underlying `ParquetDataset` provides (e.g. reading path-based partition columns into the data, supporting zero-read filter pushdown on partition columns, etc.). We do actually have a distributed analog for `pyarrow.parquet.read_table()`, and that's `ray.data.read_parquet_bulk()`, which doesn't use `pyarrow.parquet.ParquetDataset` and instead directly uses `pyarrow.parquet.read_table()`.

For this API, I think that directing users to `pyarrow.dataset.Scanner.from_fragment()` for `**arrow_parquet_args` and to `pyarrow.parquet.ParquetDataset` for `dataset_kwargs` would be best. Going forward, if we continue to build out our own format-agnostic partitioning machinery, we should eventually consider switching to `pyarrow.parquet.read_table()` if/when we achieve feature parity.

Another solution proposed by @zhoxing-ms is to supply `polling=False` to `nw_client.virtual_networks.begin_create_or_update` so that `LROPoller` is created with `NoPolling`:

https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/ad5308d9befefdc0cdc61c4a47a3329fd874b8a0/sdk/network/azure-mgmt-network/azure/mgmt/network/v2020_08_01/operations/_virtual_networks_operations.py#L340-L341

```py
if polling is True: polling_method = ARMPolling(lro_delay, lro_options={'final-state-via': 'azure-async-operation'}, path_format_arguments=path_format_arguments,  **kwargs)
elif polling is False: polling_method = NoPolling()
```
This stops SDK from polling the result. The Virtual Network creation will then be **superseded/canceled** by Subnet creation.

### Pros

- This is faster as it doesn't care about the result of Virtual Network creation.

### Cons

- This leaves the result of Virtual Network creation unchecked. If the Virtual Network creation actually fails, the error will be reported at 
  https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/blob/95d3346aeab751820eb5c09be588586cac409890/src/azure-cli/azure/cli/command_modules/rdbms/flexible_server_virtual_network.py#L188-L189
  making troubleshooting difficult.
- This depends on the service's ability to allow one operation to be superseded by another. In many other services like Storage, this is not always the case - we can't create a container before Storage Account creation finished.

> I think we should only run postProcessor on the varfont, not on the masters.

indeed, the postProcessor is not actually doing anything when the `compileInterpolatable*FromDS` funcs are being called from the respective `compileVariable{TTF,CFF2}` functions, we are deliberately turning off subroutinization (for CFF only) as well as glyph renaming (via useProductionNames=False).
We could make so that we skip the whole post-processor step when building the interpolatable master TTFs/OTFs and only run at the very end of the (static/variable) pipeline. Then we could keep a single reloadFont in the postProcessor, just before we rename glyphs or immediatley after we drop them (setting post 3.0), which should make sure the TTX dumps contain the expected names.
To keep it simple, we could change the `compileInterpolatable*FromDS` to never call the postProcessor, basically ignoring the `userProductionNames` parameter. The problem is those same functions are not only used internally by `compileVariable*`, but are also used by fontmake for the `-o {o,t}tf-interpolatable` targets and currently they do respect the `userProductionNames` parameter (even though it doesn't make much sense to do glyph renaming on these intermediate build products). Maybe one can argue that the whole point of the interpolatable binary master is to build variable fonts so ignoring the glyph renaming process should be the default and only behavior.

@pierrechevalier83 right now I'm inclined to go with @stuhood 's suggestion that was also raised in https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/issues/7907 - giving a name to an `@rule` in python is the thing that controls whether or not a workunit is even recorded (that is, whether we create a `WorkUnit` struct and then call `workunit_store().add_workunit()` on it. Obviously, this would drastically reduce the amount of information that was reported in zipkin, at least until someone went through and manually added `name=<something>` to a bunch of `@rule`s in python, so I don't want to do this without checking with you and other people currently using zipkin. But if the point of named rules is to control what rules we do and do not care about reporting information about, then I think this behavior is exactly what we want. If we went this route, I would get rid of `display_info` as a separate field on `WorkUnit`, and instead write the string from python into the existing `name` field.

Another option is that I could get rid of `display_info` as a separate field in this commit set, and instead have the `name` field on `WorkUnit` preferentially get its name from the `name=` kwarg in python on an `@rule`, and just fall back to the current behavior (which is `let node_name = format!("{}", self);` on line 1216 of `nodes.rs`) if there is no name from python available. If we do this, then naming an `@rule` in python would affect the name of a WorkUnit as reported to zipkin and/or the python-side async workunit handler, but it wouldn't affect whether or not that reporting happened. If we go this route, we would probably want to also add a separate kwarg to `@rule`s that _did_ control this reporting - maybe something like `@rule(reporting=False)\ndef my_rule(a: A) -> B:`. Whether this proposed kwarg would default to `True` or `False` also depends on what behavior the people currently using zipkin want to see.

One of the common ways of making requests via urllib3 is by using `urllib3.PoolManager().request(...)`, which abstracts the underlying connection pools from the user. As part of this abstraction, ports are assigned to the selected ConnectionPool class as a feature of the library:

https://github.com/urllib3/urllib3/blob/master/src/urllib3/poolmanager.py#L217-L221

This inferred port is finally passed to the constructor here: https://github.com/urllib3/urllib3/blob/master/src/urllib3/poolmanager.py#L268

This happens whether a port is explicitly declared in the request URL or not. 

```
>>> from ddtrace import patch
>>> patch(urllib3=True)
>>> import urllib3
>>> http = urllib3.PoolManager()
>>> r = http.request('GET', 'http://httpbin.org/robots.txt')
# In the patched function:
(Pdb) obj.port
80
```

We can monkey-patch at a higher level, detect if the port is set on the url, and set a flag on the connection pool class. But let's see if customers actually request that feature before going that route.

Yeah, the defintion of `repr()` from the [Python documentation](https://docs.python.org/2/library/functions.html#repr) seems to allow for some flexibility:

> Return a string containing a **printable representation** of an object. This is the same value yielded by conversions (reverse quotes). It is sometimes useful to be able to access this operation as an ordinary function. For **many** types, this function **makes an attempt** to return a string that would yield an object with the same value when passed to eval(), otherwise the representation is a string enclosed in angle brackets that contains the name of the type of the object together with additional information often including the name and address of the object. A class can control what this function returns for its instances by defining a __repr__() method.

Emphasis mine. My thoughts:
- "printable representation" makes sense for a string with redacted, sensitive information that we might want to "print" (in this case, to tracing)
- "many" types, "makes an attempt" seem to imply that it's not an absolute requirement that `__repr__` be able to be passed to `eval()` to reconstruct an object.

I could go either way. I think the use in this context makes sense, but I also think calling `__to_string__` would probably reasonable as it doesn't seem *private* as much as a weirdly-named magic method.

@dougleaf , thanks for this PR.

I don't favor disabling the default for a specific log reagion (e.g. `LOG_MAC` region)  in the oepnthread repo.  I think the default should be the same for all different log regions.

 It can be of course controlled by application/product specifc OT config files (if there is a need).

In general, I suggest using "log level" insetad. Here is high-level use (in some of our internal project/products)
- `LOG_LEVEL_DEBG` is mainly used in special build by developers/engineers for debugging only (it's really verbose)
   - I recommend using `DEBG` level only in special case/build  (e.g. debugging radio driver issues, etc).
- `LOG_LEVEL_INFO` or `LOG_LEVEL_NOTE` are commonly used during development/intergation of OT and for in-field testing:
   - `NOTE` level commonly logs recoverable failure cases (e.g. dropping a message, failing to recv ack after retx, etc).
   -  `INFO` provides more info and logs even successful interactions (e.g. successful msg send or receive) - which are useful during integration/debugging (this is in addition to `NOTE` level logs).
   
- On release firmware, the log level is decided based on capability of device. On more capable devices `INFO` or `NOTE` may be used but often the logs are fully disabled, or `WARN` level is used.
- We also have dynamic log level feature which when used, allows log level to be changed during run-time.

MinSequence in RFC 7731 does not necessarily map to the minimum sequence received. Instead, MinSequence is intended to indicate the minimum sequence that the device is willing to receive.

This PR technically only implements the MinSequence concept when there are no available entries. This makes sense because entries may be evicted before the lifetime, so the MinSequence rule helps avoid forwarding loops.

When there are entries available, the entry lifetime should be such that forwarding loops are not possible. When the lifetime expires for all entries, that is equivalent to the RFC 7731 Seed Set entry lifetime expiring. At that point, any Sequence value will be accepted again.

I think this may be prone to duplication of work being overlooked when making code changes at further point in time since passing `None` for the `module` argument will be silently handled by `cloudpickle`. On the other hand, this solution may be useful if we actually need an "atomic" `_is_importable` check at some point in cloudpickle. I am pretty sure we do not need it, and that each time we are calling `_is_importable` right now in cloudpickle, we are actually checking for authorization to pickle by value, and not actual importability. Feel free to double check - if you confirm that, then I'm -0.5 on the solution because of the risk of work duplication.  If actual importability checks are needed, then this solution may achieve the best readability/efficiency tradeoff. 

An alternative fix is to rename `_is_importable` to `_should_pickle_by_value`, and:
- add a comment above the `lookup_module_and_qualname` call inside it precising that this call is a proxy to check importability of `obj`
- include a `is_registered_pickle_by_value` check inside `_should_pickle_by_value` that will use as input the module name returned as part of the result of the `_lookup_qualname_and_module` call made prior to this check.

> It turned out this line was a bug. The `safe_copy` tries to use hard linking, and when that succeeds, you can get a symlink out the other side if `f` went in as a symlink (hardlink of a symlink on linux is a symlink). Wrapping the LHS with an os.path.realpath fixes and lets --spread be used over in Pants where it nets a ~23x space savings vs main.

It looks like sandbox outputs are currently symlink aware (because they don't use `PosixFS::new_with_symlink_behavior(..., SymlinkBehavior:Oblivous)` [here](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/70f0cd78db860b86c8a611e658e745dffa3f04fc/src/rust/engine/process_execution/src/local.rs#L583-L599)), but that could be disabled if we think that it aligns with expected REAPI behavior? cc @tdyas

First let's disable all monkeypatching with a small change in our [patching fixture](https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest-flask/blob/master/pytest_flask/plugin.py#L81):
```python
@pytest.fixture(autouse=True)
def _monkeypatch_response_class(request, monkeypatch):
 
    return # return here, don't do anything

    if "app" not in request.fixturenames:
        return
    # ...
```
Now, let's see what kind of object we get with a little introspection on [test_json_response.py](https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest-flask/pull/133/files#diff-ae1ce22fcb54fdef8c463925740ee8ab50e72eb1305724f0d8309afb84e0d0c6R7):
```python
class TestJSONResponse:
    def test_json_response(self, client, accept_json):
        res = client.get(url_for("ping"), headers=accept_json)
        print(type(res))
        # ...
```
so, `res ` is a `<class 'flask.wrappers.Response'>`. If we check the [source code of this class](https://github.com/pallets/flask/blob/master/src/flask/wrappers.py#L90), we can see it's just a wrapper for [werkzeug.wrappers.Response](https://github.com/pallets/werkzeug/blob/master/src/werkzeug/wrappers/response.py#L64)

Let's check its [json](https://github.com/pallets/werkzeug/blob/master/src/werkzeug/wrappers/response.py#L637) property

```python
@property
def json(self) -> t.Optional[t.Any]:
    # ...
    return self.get_json()
```
and the [get_json](https://github.com/pallets/werkzeug/blob/master/src/werkzeug/wrappers/response.py#L645) method
```python
def get_json(self, force: bool = False, silent: bool = False) -> t.Optional[t.Any]:
    if not (force or self.is_json):
        return None

    data = self.get_data()

    try:
        return self.json_module.loads(data)    #  <---- loading json into python object
    except ValueError:
        if not silent:
            raise

        return None
```
In `return self.json_module.loads(data)`, `self.json_module` is actually python's native `json` module. We can see `json_module = json` a [little above](https://github.com/pallets/werkzeug/blob/master/src/werkzeug/wrappers/response.py#L634). So the method is basically applying https://docs.python.org/3/library/json.html#json.loads to the json object.

Finally, let's double check the response object returned by the test client just to be sure
```python
class TestJSONResponse:
    def test_json_response(self, client, accept_json):
        res = client.get(url_for("ping"), headers=accept_json)
        print(inspect.getsource(res.get_json))
        # ...
```
and
```python
def get_json(self, force=False, silent=False, cache=True):
        """Parse :attr:`data` as JSON.
        # ...
```

I encountered the same problem while implementing the POST-as-GET protocol. Pebble by default will also reject a percentage of valid nonce during POST requests. Certbot has a recover mechanism to reissue a new nonce in this case, but not two.

It was working before POST-as-GET, but with it, the number of POST done during a certificate issuance increased of an order of magnitude. Then the probability that two nonces are rejected at a time, and so the recover mechanism fails, increased sufficiently to fail 1 out of 3 integration tests run in the certbot CI.

We hesitated between adding more tries to reduce again the probability to an acceptable rate, or disable the feature Pebble-side with the relevant env variable to get a completely reproducible behavior. 

We went with the second option finally: https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/118cb3c9b1e62599b57082b71ab7313b2cdc1632/certbot-ci/certbot_integration_tests/utils/acme_server.py#L127

I figured it out. It it because [`execl`](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/cpp/c-runtime-library/exec-wexec-functions?view=vs-2019) internally calls [CreateProcessW ](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/win32/api/processthreadsapi/nf-processthreadsapi-createprocessw):

https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/cpp/c-runtime-library/exec-wexec-functions?view=vs-2019

> Spaces embedded in strings may cause unexpected behavior; for example, passing `_exec` the string `"hi there"` will result in the new process getting two arguments, `"hi"` and `"there"`. If the intent was to have the new process open a file named "hi there", the process would fail. You can avoid this by quoting the string: `"\"hi there\""`.

[`subprocess.Popen`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/subprocess.html#subprocess.Popen) can handle escaping correctly by converting `args` to a string in a manner described in [Converting an argument sequence to a string on Windows](https://docs.python.org/3/library/subprocess.html#converting-argument-sequence). The implementation is [`subprocess.list2cmdline`](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/1def7754b7a41fe57efafaf5eff24cfa15353444/Lib/subprocess.py#L529).

```python
from typing_extensions import Literal
from typing import Any


def my_func(x: Literal['a', 'b']) -> str:
    return x + x


x: Any = 'a'
my_func(x) # ok
y: Any = 'c'
my_func(y) # ok even if wrong value, but with type Any it passes
z: str = 'a'
my_func(z) # not ok because type is str and not Literal
```

Consider the following:

```python
import lightgbm as lgb
from sklearn.datasets import load_breast_cancer

X, y = load_breast_cancer(return_X_y=True)
data = lgb.Dataset(X, label=y)

# train model
clf = lgb.LGBMClassifier(
    objective="binary",
    n_estimators=5
)

clf.fit(X, y)
clf._Booster = None
clf.n_estimators_
```

On `master`, that code will yield this error:

> AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'current_iteration'

As of this PR, it would return this error

> No n_estimators found. Need to call fit beforehand.

So this is not a "false positive" from `mypy`. `mypy` is correctly complaining about the fact that this code path and others like it treat `self._Booster` as non-None even though it is possible for it to be `None`.

This is also why I don't see the proposed change here as "nonsense". With the change proposed on this line, in the unlikely and unsupported but **possible** scenario where `_Booster` has been set to `None`, you'll get a more informative error message.

Hardly anyone needs support for all ISO-8601 formats, and the full spec is pretty challenging to implement (so no one in the python eco-system got it right as far as I know).

Yes, you could generate schemas with:

```JSON
{
    "$schema": "https://spec.openapis.org/oas/3.1/dialect/base",
    ...
}
```
instead of the standard 2020-12 dialect meta-schema, which includes all of standard 2020-12 plus the four [OpenAPI dialect extension keywords](https://spec.openapis.org/oas/latest.html#dialectSchemaId): `discriminator`, `xml`, `externalDocs`, and `example` (singular, deprecated in favor of the standard `examples`, plural).

Of those four, `discriminator` might be the only one that's relevant to you, but it is used fairly heavily in OpenAPI.  It will likely be removed in OpenAPI 4 in favor of a standard JSON Schema keyword or keywords that will work a bit differently.  `discriminator` doesn't quite fit the standard JSON Schema processing model.

1) either we follow lxml method names (.remove and .drop_tree), or 
2) we try to have better names - have Selector.remove behave like .drop_tree, not like lxml's .remove.

Following lxml names could be less surprising for people familiar with lxml, and it'd also be backwards compatible. Changing .remove behavior could be less surprising for people in general, but could create some confusion for people who use lxml, and it's a backwards incompatible change.

Both approaches look fine to me.

3: I think the `initializer` is necessary with the current design, but a maybe not with a different design.

With `functools.reduce(function, iterable, initializer)`, the initializer is optional because you can infer it. If `initializer` isn't provided, it's set to the return value of the first `function` call. 

But `raster_reduce` has two parts: it aggregates each block to a single value and then reduces _those_ values to a single value. In the current implementation, the `function` does both of those operations. For instance with the histogram example, the correct value of `initializer` to infer would be the result of `numpy.histogram` called on the first block. But there's no way to know that because `numpy.histogram` is wrapped up in the `function`.

But it would be possible if we split out the block aggregation and reducing operations into separate functions, something like:
```
def raster_reduce(raster_path, block_aggregate_fn, raster_reduce_fn, initializer=None):
    # reduce each block to a single value
    block_values = map(block_aggregate_fn, iterblocks(raster_path))
    if initializer is not None:
        block_values = [initializer] + block_values
    # reduce that series of block values to a single value
    return functools.reduce(raster_reduce_fn , block_values)
```
Then the histogram example could be written as
```
def block_histogram(block): return numpy.histogram(block, bins=10)[0]
def sum(x, y): return x + y
raster_reduce(raster_path, block_histogram, sum)
```

I think I get your point. But not in favor of making `_dispatch()` private -- as that's public API as far as `HTTPMiddleware` is concerned. It feels odd to be required to override a private method.

Library authors who do really want to hide a dispatch implementation from their users could always do this:

```python
class MyScopedMiddleware:
    def __init__(self, app):
        self._app = HTTPMiddleware(app, dispatch=self._dispatch)

    async def _dispatch(self, __conn):
        ...

    async def __call__(self, scope, receive, send):
        await self._app(scope, receive, send)
```

Right, non-nan and nan cases produce different shapes of the aggregations.

For non-nan cases it's enough to compute plain sums/sums of squares/num of observations, all of them producing Series'ses:
```python
# non-nan case
>>> df
   a  b  c
0  1  3  5
1  2  3  4
2  2  4  3
3  3  5  2
# simply computing sum for each column, that's enough to compute
# proper correlation matrix
>>> df.sum() 
a     8
b    15
c    14
dtype: int64
```

In a NaN case the handling is much more complex as we need special NaN-handling logic [described here](https://github.com/dchigarev/modin/blob/035aadddcf900576fa98a8fb39b564de258e68c2/modin/core/storage_formats/pandas/aggregations.py#L259-L274):
```python
# nan-case
>>> df_nan
     a    b  c
0  1.0  NaN  5
1  2.0  3.0  4
2  NaN  4.0  3
3  3.0  5.0  2
# we have to compute pairwise sums excluding NaN values from each column individually
>>> compute_nan_aggs(df_nan)["sum"]
     a     b     c
a  6.0   8.0  11.0
b  5.0  12.0   9.0
c  6.0  12.0  14.0
```

It's not a problem if different partitions of the same frame would produce different-shaped results, as [it's possible to align them at the Reduce phase](https://github.com/dchigarev/modin/blob/035aadddcf900576fa98a8fb39b564de258e68c2/modin/core/storage_formats/pandas/aggregations.py#L402-L421)

The only missing detail is a very core thing in a POSIX system: there is no requirement for absolute or relative file paths in general, these are only imposed by third party software if at all. All filesystem operations always account for CWD and if a path is absolute, ignore it or if a path is relative, their resolved by joining with CWD. So `absolute` was a general conceptual red-herring of a requirement here.

So, while attempting to run `fmt` on this branch after adjusting the timeouts, I encountered two issues here:

1. This line will match all `package.json` files in a repository, regardless of whether they have had `package_json` targets have been tailored for them. That means that it finds the `package.json` file that we have in `docs` (which, for now, we're fine having ignored). Instead, what you probably want to do is request `AllTargets`, and then filter them to the appropriate type: e.g. https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/213b19fa23f9281f4f43a63f03ac02f3c4825ff1/src/python/pants/jvm/dependency_inference/artifact_mapper.py#L255-L259
2. `docs/package.json` does not have a `name` or `version` field, which causes `parse_package_json` to fail with a keyerror, since it considers them to be required. AFAICT from https://docs.npmjs.com/cli/v9/configuring-npm/package-json, `name` and `version` are only required if you intend to publish a package.

Just fixing 1 is likely sufficient, but it would probably be good to improve the error for 2.

To build docstrings of `cuDF` engine files using autodoc we need to install `cudf` and `cupy` conda packages from nVidia channels ([link](https://github.com/rapidsai/cudf#conda)). I excluded this way for now by the next reasons:

1. The size of all installed packages is about 2GB.
2. nVidia GPU driver is require to install (possible, issues will be occured on the server without GPU).

On further consideration, I believe the tests already address this concern and here's how:

- [create_package](https://github.com/python/importlib_resources/blob/54a61c457df31c4e0e3ac38aa6ebc6fe1acb671d/importlib_resources/tests/util.py#L63-L67) in util creates a Loader/Reader that [does not implement `.files()`](https://github.com/python/importlib_resources/blob/54a61c457df31c4e0e3ac38aa6ebc6fe1acb671d/importlib_resources/tests/util.py#L17-L51).
- For that loader/reader [tests assert](https://github.com/python/importlib_resources/blob/54a61c457df31c4e0e3ac38aa6ebc6fe1acb671d/importlib_resources/tests/test_compatibilty_files.py#L87-L89) that a `CompatibilityFiles` is loaded by `wrap_spec()` (as found in _adapters).
- [files() calls wrap_spec](https://github.com/python/importlib_resources/blob/54a61c457df31c4e0e3ac38aa6ebc6fe1acb671d/importlib_resources/_common.py#L18-L23), which [on CPython is `_adapters.wrap_spec`](https://github.com/python/importlib_resources/blob/d89e03e3800a4baf50e3f3820754046971d04ae7/Lib/importlib/_common.py#L12).

> To support this use-case, I suspect we'll want to move the `_native_reader() or CompatibilityFiles()` logic to `_common.from_package`

- The `_native_reader() or CompatibilityFiles()` [logic is present](https://github.com/python/importlib_resources/blob/d89e03e3800a4baf50e3f3820754046971d04ae7/Lib/importlib/_adapters.py#L63-L68), similar to how I was imagining:

> Consider implementing this compatibility shim as a `CompatibilityFiles.try_native` classmethod.

At this point, I no longer have any concerns about the compatibility shim not being active in the stdlib.

A few improvement:
- All resource related internal data structures should be delegated to ActorPool or any successor of PG Manager, instead of spreading across RayTrialExecutor and PGManager. For example, `RayTrialExecutor._cached_actor_pg`, `PGManager._cached_pgs` alike should be in one place. This is easier to keep consistency (so that we request exactly what we need...)
- Bullet proof support of heterogeneous trials. Our current code mostly likely is not going to work in its current state.
- Unit testing... we don't have test for placement group manager, partially because we don't have a good API. Test today is done with Executor together.

There are more details here: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1RDsW7SVzwMPZfA0WLOPA4YTqbRyXIHGYmBenJk33HaE/edit#heading=h.rb8vp3b62vu2
